## Governing Equations

### 1 · Gurney: Initial Fragment Velocity

For a cylindrical shell the Gurney formula gives initial fragment velocity $V_0$:

$$
V_0 = \sqrt{2E} \left(\frac{M}{C} + \tfrac{1}{2}\right)^{-1/2} \quad (1)
$$

| Symbol      | Description                                  | Unit |
| ----------- | -------------------------------------------- | ---- |
| $\sqrt{2E}$ | Gurney energy constant (explosive-dependent) | m/s  |
| $M$         | Metal (shell body) mass                      | kg   |
| $C$         | Explosive filler mass                        | kg   |
| $V_0$       | Initial fragment velocity                    | m/s  |

Gurney constants: 2 440 m/s (TNT), 2 700 m/s (Comp-B), 2 930 m/s (RDX).

### 2 · Shell Geometry at Break-up

The shell expands under detonation pressure before fracturing. Pearson (1990) and
the PAFRAG methodology set break-up at approximately three times the initial internal
volume of explosive products (V/V₀ ≈ 3). For a cylindrical geometry the inner
radius grows as:

$$
r_{i,\text{bu}} = r_{i,0}\,\sqrt{3} \quad (2a)
$$

Assuming the steel wall is incompressible (volume conserved during radial expansion):

$$
r_{o,\text{bu}} = \sqrt{r_{i,\text{bu}}^2 + (r_{o,0}^2 - r_{i,0}^2)} \quad (2b)
$$

The Mott formula uses the **mean** shell radius at break-up:

$$
r_{\text{bu}} = \tfrac{1}{2}(r_{i,\text{bu}} + r_{o,\text{bu}}) \quad (2c)
$$

| Symbol                                  | Description                   | Unit |
| --------------------------------------- | ----------------------------- | ---- |
| $r_{i,0}$, $r_{o,0}$                   | Initial inner/outer radii     | m    |
| $r_{i,\text{bu}}$, $r_{o,\text{bu}}$ | Inner/outer radii at break-up | m    |
| $r_{\text{bu}}$                        | Mean shell radius at break-up | m    |

### 3 · Mott: Fragment Mass Distribution

At break-up, stress-release waves arrest further fracture in their wake (Mott 1947).
The cumulative fragment count is:

$$
N(m) = N_0 \, \exp\!\left(-\sqrt{m / \mu}\right) \quad (3)
$$

where $N_0 = M_{\text{shell}} / (2\mu)$ and the half-weight $\mu$ (half the average
fragment mass) from the PAFRAG-Mott formulation (Gold 2017, eq. 16) is:

$$
\mu = \sqrt{\frac{2}{\rho}}\left(\frac{\sigma_F}{\gamma}\right)^{3/2}
\left(\frac{r_{\text{bu}}}{V_0}\right)^3 \quad (4)
$$

This is algebraically identical to the form $\frac{1}{2}(2\sigma_F/\rho^{1/3}\gamma)^{3/2}(r/V_0)^3$;
equation (4) matches the PAFRAG source notation exactly.

| Symbol          | Description                              | Unit  | Value (105 mm M1) |
| --------------- | ---------------------------------------- | ----- | ----------------- |
| $\rho$         | Shell steel density                      | kg/m³ | 7 850             |
| $\sigma_F$     | Dynamic flow stress at fracture          | Pa    | 800 × 10⁶         |
| $\gamma$       | Mott statistical fragmentation parameter | —     | 65                |
| $r_{\text{bu}}$| Mean shell radius at break-up            | m     | computed          |
| $\mu$          | Half the average fragment mass           | kg    | computed          |

**Note on $\gamma$ and $\sigma_F$:** These are the dominant uncertainties.
Mott (1947) Table 1 gives γ ≈ 53 for 0.2%C steel and γ ≈ 67 for 0.3%C steel.
US M1 shell bodies use SAE 1045 steel (≈ 0.45%C); γ ≈ 65 is a central estimate.
Dynamic flow stress at high strain rate is typically 800–1 000 MPa for hardened steel,
higher than the quasi-static value of 600 MPa. The sensitivity section explores both.

The most probable fragment mass (mode of the number-weighted PDF) is $m_{\text{mode}} = \mu/4$.

### 4 · Drag: Velocity Decay Along Flat Trajectory

For a fragment of mass $m$ flying horizontally, dividing the drag equation of motion by $v$:

$$
m \frac{dv}{ds} = -\tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho_{\text{air}}\,C_D\,A_f\,v \quad (5)
$$

This integrates to exponential decay:

$$
v(s) = V_0\,e^{-\lambda s}, \qquad
\lambda(m) = \frac{\rho_{\text{air}}\,C_D\,C_{\text{shape}}}{2\,\rho^{2/3}}\,m^{-1/3} \quad (6)
$$

where the mean presented area of a tumbling irregular fragment scales as
$A_f = C_{\text{shape}}(m/\rho)^{2/3}$, so smaller fragments decelerate faster.

| Symbol              | Description                                      | Unit  | Value |
| ------------------- | ------------------------------------------------ | ----- | ----- |
| $\rho_{\text{air}}$| Air density (sea level)                         | kg/m³ | 1.225 |
| $C_D$               | Drag coefficient, tumbling fragment              | —     | 0.65  |
| $C_{\text{shape}}$ | Mean presented-area factor vs. equal-volume cube | —     | 0.90  |

### 5 · Kinetic Energy and Fragmentation Field

Kinetic energy at distance $s$ from burst:

$$
E_k(s,m) = \tfrac{1}{2}\,m\,V_0^2\,e^{-2\lambda(m)\,s} \quad (7)
$$

The Mott number-density PDF (fragments per unit mass interval):

$$
n(m) = \frac{N_0}{2\sqrt{\mu\,m}}\,\exp\!\left(-\sqrt{m/\mu}\right) \quad (8)
$$

The ES-310 lethality curve $P_{k|\text{hit}}(E)$ is a log-linear interpolation between
three anchors — 100 J → 0.10, 1 kJ → 0.50, 4 kJ → 0.90 — clipped to $[0,\,0.90]$.

Expected lethal hits on a target of presented width $w$ at distance $r$
(disk geometry, azimuthally uniform spray):

$$
N_{\text{eff}}(r) = \int_0^\infty n(m)\;P_{k|\text{hit}}\!\left(E_k(r,m)\right)\frac{w}{2\pi r}\,dm \quad (9)
$$

Kill probability (Poisson, single target):

$$
P_{\text{kill}}(r) = 1 - \exp\!\left(-N_{\text{eff}}(r)\right) \quad (10)
$$

$R_{50}$ is the distance at which $P_{\text{kill}} = 0.5$.

### 6 · Burst Geometry and Height of Burst

Equations (1)–(10) describe the fragment field as a function of *slant range*
$s$ from the burst point, with no reference to where the burst sits relative
to the ground or where the fragments land. The disk geometry of eq. (9)
implicitly assumes an azimuthally uniform spray intersecting a vertical
target at the same height as the burst. Real artillery bursts occur at a
finite **height of burst** $h_b$, and the shell arrives along a sloped
trajectory. Both effects break the disk symmetry and govern how the
fragment field is mapped onto the ground. The equations below are the
foundation for the four-zone ground projection (§6.5, eq. 13–14), where
the implementation lives in `arty.zones`.

(Equation numbers (11)–(14) are reserved by the four-zone section; the new
relations introduced here are numbered from (15).)

#### Slant range and arrival elevation

For a burst at height $h_b$ above flat ground, a ground patch at horizontal
offset $(x_g, y_g)$ from the burst's ground track lies at slant range

$$
s = \sqrt{x_g^2 + y_g^2 + h_b^2} \quad (15)
$$

A fragment reaching that patch travelled along the burst-to-patch line, so
it arrives at an **elevation angle** $\gamma$ below the horizontal given by

$$
\sin\gamma = \frac{h_b}{s} \quad (16)
$$

Directly under the burst ($x_g = y_g = 0$) the arrival is vertical
($\gamma = 90°$); far out on the ground ($\sqrt{x_g^2+y_g^2}\gg h_b$) the
arrival grazes the surface ($\gamma \to 0$). This $\gamma$ is exactly the
arrival angle fed to the presented-area model $A_p(\gamma,\text{posture})$
(§6.6).

| Symbol           | Description                                   | Unit |
| ---------------- | --------------------------------------------- | ---- |
| $h_b$            | Height of burst above ground                  | m    |
| $(x_g, y_g)$     | Ground-patch offset from burst ground track   | m    |
| $s$              | Slant range, burst point to ground patch      | m    |
| $\gamma$         | Fragment arrival elevation angle below horizontal | rad/° |

#### Ground footprint scaling

A fragment leaving the burst point $(0,0,h_b)$ along unit velocity
$(\hat v_{g,x}, \hat v_{g,y}, \hat v_{g,z})$ with downward component
$\hat v_{g,z} < 0$ strikes the ground at

$$
x_\text{hit} = -\frac{\hat v_{g,x}}{\hat v_{g,z}}\,h_b, \qquad
y_\text{hit} = -\frac{\hat v_{g,y}}{\hat v_{g,z}}\,h_b \quad (= \text{eq. }14)
$$

(this is eq. 14 of §6.5, restated; the direction cosines come from the AoF
rotation, eq. 13a–c). Because $h_b$ multiplies both coordinates, the entire
ground footprint **scales linearly with burst height**: doubling $h_b$
doubles every hit distance and quadruples the footprint area, while leaving
the angular structure of the spray untouched. Height of burst is therefore
the single knob that trades footprint *size* against fragment *density* and
arrival *angle*.

#### Ground burst versus airburst

At $h_b = 0$ (contact burst) eq. (14) collapses: every fragment with a
downward component hits within a few metres of the impact point. Lethality
is intense but the lethal area is tiny — much of the spray is wasted into
the ground or over the heads of nearby targets. As $h_b$ increases the
footprint expands (linearly, above) and the *average* arrival angle
$\gamma$ falls: fragments reach a given ground patch more obliquely.

The arrival angle controls how much of the target a fragment "sees". For a
standing target the presented silhouette combines a frontal term that
scales with $\cos\gamma$ and an overhead term that scales with $\sin\gamma$:
schematically $A_p \sim A_f\cos\gamma + A_t\sin\gamma$, with $A_f$ the
frontal and $A_t$ the plan (overhead) silhouette. A steep overhead burst
($\gamma\to 90°$) presents only the small plan area $A_t$ — the standing
target is well protected; a grazing arrival ($\gamma\to 0$) presents the
full frontal area $A_f$ — the target is maximally exposed. This is the
mechanism encoded in `presented_area(γ, posture)` in `arty.fragmentation`
(§6.6, eq. 22′), and it is why optimal HOB is a compromise: high enough to
spread the footprint and lower $\gamma$ onto standing targets, low enough
that density has not thinned below the lethal threshold.

#### Angle of fall (AoF)

The shell arrives along its trajectory at **angle of fall** AoF measured
from horizontal: AoF $= 0°$ is a horizontal trajectory, AoF $= 90°$ a
vertical plunge. AoF tilts the entire spray cone via the rotation
$R_y(\text{AoF})$ (eq. 13a–c), breaking the azimuthal symmetry that eq. (9)
assumes. Two limits make the effect concrete:

- **AoF $= 0°$ (flat trajectory).** The equatorial (cylinder) spray forms a
  vertical ring around the burst. Fragments going exactly sideways are
  horizontal and never reach the ground; the downward-going half of the ring
  does land, but projects to a transverse strip at $x = 0$ directly below the
  burst — not a forward footprint. The extended forward and rearward ground
  coverage comes from the ogive and base/boattail zones, whose spray cones
  are tilted fore and aft of the equatorial plane.
- **AoF $= 90°$ (vertical plunge).** The spray is azimuthally symmetric
  about the vertical. Each zone's cone projects to a clean ring; a zone
  with spray half-angle $\theta^z$ from the (now downward) shell axis
  strikes a ring of radius

$$
r_\text{ring} = h_b\,\tan\theta^z \quad (17)
$$

  Per-zone angles $\theta^z$ are defined in §6.1; the ogive ring
  spot-check in §6.5 verifies eq. (17) numerically.

For realistic indirect fire the AoF is intermediate. WW2 105 mm howitzer
fire at 4–6 km range falls at AoF $\approx 20°$–$35°$, so the spray cone is
only modestly tilted: the forward sector of the footprint receives
substantially more fragment density than the rear, and the symmetric-disk
model of eq. (9) is a **conservative** (rear-biased, density-averaged)
approximation of the true lobed field that the four-zone projection (§6.7)
reproduces.

| Symbol               | Description                                      | Unit |
| -------------------- | ------------------------------------------------ | ---- |
| AoF                  | Angle of fall, trajectory below horizontal (0°=flat, 90°=plunge) | ° |
| $(x_\text{hit}, y_\text{hit})$ | Ground-impact coordinates of a fragment | m    |
| $\theta^z$           | Per-zone spray half-angle from shell axis (§6.1) | °    |
| $r_\text{ring}$      | Ground ring radius for symmetric (AoF = 90°) burst | m  |

______________________________________________________________________
